Summary: Using API functions Simulink.Bus.save and Simulink.Bus.objectToCell with bus objects that have n-dimensional (n > 2) bus elements may result in loss of data
Description: If a bus object contains any elements with dimensions greater than 2-D, the API functions Simulink.Bus.save  and Simulink.Bus.objectToCell  will not save the dimensions correctly. The dimensions field for any n-dimensional bus elements will be erroneously truncated to 1-D. For example, if a Bus Object contains a signal with dimensions [3 3 3], the API function Simulink.Bus.save  will truncate the dimensions to 3. When the bus objects are restored from the generated file, the dimensions will be incorrect.
Workaround: Open the file generated by the API function and edit all incorrect dimensions fields to have the correct values.

Summary: Callbacks of the axes in a GUIDE GUI are cleared and its other properties are changed after using graphics commands
Description: When creating a plot in a GUIDE GUI axes, the design time properties of the axes, including its callbacks, can change when the plot is created. Resetting axes properties is standard behavior for most high-level MATLAB plotting functions. In addition, the plot can be created in an axes other than the one intended.
Workaround: To ensure that you plot into the correct axes when you create a plot in a GUIDE GUI, explicitly specify the handle of the target axes to the plot command. The handle is available from the handles structure provided with each GUIDE generated callback sub-function. Most plot functions enable you to specify the handle as an argument, for example,plot(handles.axes1,...); plots into the axes with handle handles.axes1.If your graphics function does not allow for a handle argument, check the function reference page for information on specifying the target axes. For example, image lets you specify a Parent property.image(C,'Parent',handles.axes1);creates the image in the target axes with handle handles.axes1, specified by the Parent property.By default, many graphics commands clear the axes and/or its children. This resets all properties of the target axes. If you want to retain the properties, you can change the axes NextPlot property to get the behavior you want.This example shows you how to display an image in your axes while retaining the axes properties:[X,map] = imread('trees.tif');set(handles.axes1,'NextPlot','replacechildren')handles.ih = image(X, 'Parent',handles.axes1);set(handles.axes1,'ydir','reverse');dim =size(X);set(handles.axes1,'xlim',[0,dim(2)-1]);set(handles.axes1,'ylim',[0,dim(1)-1]);

Summary: Slider value is incorrect when its Units property is set to inches, centimeters, pixels, or characters.
Description: If a slider's Units  property is set to inches , centimeters , pixels  or characters , the slider reports its maximum value (Max  property) as its value (Value  property), regardless of the slider position.

Summary: munlock tries to unlock the first file it matches, even though this may not be the file that which finds.
Description: Because munlock  is able to unlock files that are no longer on the path, it finds its match in a different way than the which  function does. This can be confusing. Furthermore, munlock  attempts to unlock only the one match, even if it is not currently locked, and even if there is a second match. The mislocked  function acts similarly.
Workaround: To be certain you are unlocking the intended file, include the parent directory in the file specification. You do not need to use the full path.You may be able to predict ambiguity by using either of the following commands:   which -all   inmem -completenamesTheir outputs may be misleading, however, because which finds only items that are on the path, and inmem finds only those files that are loaded. The munlock function finds an item that may not be on the path, and may not even be loaded. The mislocked function behaves in a similar manner.

Summary: The hypergeom function of type 2F1 can return wrong results.
Description: Some calls to hypergeom  of type 2F1 can return wrong results. For example: hypergeom([10 30], 20, 0.5) ans = 3.5253e+015 Another example is: hypergeom([100 300], 200, 0.5) ans = -1.0556e+181
Workaround: As a workaround, use the vpa function:hypergeom([10 30], 20, vpa(0.5))ans = 74080.416249018347968872706503891For the second example, the correct result is:hypergeom([100 300], 200, vpa(0.5))ans = .67420292009486291246382060578901e49

Summary: Incorrect behavior for slider with extreme Min and Max property values
Description: If the values of the Min  and Max  properties of a uicontrol of style slider  are of the order of 105  and larger or 10-5  and smaller, then the slider can fail to operate correctly.

Summary: When using ModelSim 6.2 or later, use the -novopt flag when invoking the ModelSim vsim command
Description: In ModelSim 6.2 or later versions, ModelSim removes signals that are automatically added to the waveform viewer. This can cause problems when you execute simulation scripts generated by Simulink HDL Coder. You must use the -novopt  flag when invoking the ModelSim vsim  command, if you are using ModelSim 6.2 or later versions.
Workaround:
The default setting for the HDLSimCmd property is: 'vsim work.%s
' When using ModelSim 6.2 and beyond, you should change this to: 'vsim -novopt work.%s
' To accomplish this, add the following to your control file: c.set('HDLSimCmd', 'vsim -novopt work.%s
'); Or use the makehdl function in MATLAB: makehdl('modelname/HDLSubsystem', 'HDLSimCmd', 'vsim -novopt work.%s
');

Summary: Using get or set functions on Java? objects and some Handle Graphics? objects can result in memory and performance problems
Description: Using the MATLAB? get  and set  functions on Java objects can result in out of memory: heap space errors and slower performance. MATLAB get  and set  functions are not intended or supported for use with Java objects, but rather are for use with MATLAB Handle Graphics objects. When you use get  and set  on Java objects, MATLAB implicitly creates a Handle Graphics object to wrap the Java object. The following situations would result in the problem: Using MATLAB get  or set  to get or set Java Bean properties from a Java object, such as     p = get(javaObject,'Property') Using MATLAB set  to set callbacks to MATLAB for a Java object, such as     h = java.awt.Button('click me');    set(h,'actionPerformedCallback',@(o,e) disp('hi')) Using MATLAB get  or set  to get or set Handle Graphics object properties for a Handle Graphics object that was implicitly created when get  or set  were used on a Java object.
Workaround: Use the workaround that corresponds to the source of the problem:With Java Bean PropertiesIf you are using MATLAB get or set to get or set Java Bean properties from a Java object, instead use the get or set methods that directly correspond to the Bean property.Instead of thisp = get(javaObject,'Property')use thisp = javaObject.getProperty()Setting Callbacks to MATLABIf you are using MATLAB set to set callbacks to MATLAB for a Java object, explicitly wrap the Java object within a handle.Instead of thish = java.awt.Button('click me'); set(h,'actionPerformedCallback',@(o,e) disp('hi'))use thish = java.awt.Button('click me'); hh = handle(h,'callbackProperties'); set(hh,'actionPerformedCallback',@(o,e) disp('hi'))Set or Get Properties for an Implicitly-Created Handle Graphics ObjectIf you are using MATLAB get or set to get or set Handle Graphics object properties for a Handle Graphics object that was implcitly created when get or set were used on a Java object, there is no known workaround at this time.

Summary: RTDX demos do not run on the C6727 PADK platform
Description: Configuration affected -- Code Composer Studio 3.1 with the C6727 PADK target. RTDX-based demos fail when you attempt to run them on the C6727 PADK platform.

Summary: Discrete-Time Integrator can exhibit unexpected jumps in its output
Description: A Discrete-Time Integrator can exhibit unexpected jumps in its output if the integrator inherits its sample time and resides in an Enabled Subsystem that resets its states when enabled. This is because such an integrator updates its elapsed time inconsistently. At the first enable time, the elapsed time is the enable time itself. For all subsequent enable times, the elapsed time is reset to zero. Because the first enable time can be significant, the integrator output may jump significantly at the first subsystem enable. This effect may be more pronounced when the Forward Euler integration method or the option Use initial condition as initial and reset value for  is set to State only (most efficient) . See the documentation on the Discrete-Time Integrator to determine the impact of the elapsed time on the integrator operation.
Workaround: If the Discrete-Time Integrator is to run periodically, consider rearchitecting the model so that the integrator uses an explicit sample time.Otherwise, consider making the parent Enabled Subsystem go though an artificial enable/disable cycle before the first valid use of the Discrete-Time Integrator output.

Summary: Inconsistent management of map axes "map" and "frame" limits in functions axesm and setm
Description: The map limit properties ('MapLatLimit'  and 'MapLonLimit' ) and frame limit properties ('FLatLimit'  and 'FLonLimit' ) of a map axes are interdependent. The map and frame limit properties also interact with the 'Origin'  and 'MapProjection'  properties, as well as with the "read-only" 'TrimLat'  and 'TrimLon'  properties associated with a given map projection. Because map limits are geographic/absolute and frame limits are relative to the map projection origin and both provide useful descriptions of the map extent, these interdependencies are essential. However, the inconsistent way in which they are currently implemented and documented causes serious usability problems. One problem is that you can set a given property with axesm  at the time you construct your map axes or you can set its value later with a call to setm , but in many cases these two approaches give results that differ in unexpected ways. Another problem is a complicated and inconsistent coupling between the map limits and frame limits. Together, these problems make the task of creating a map spanning a specific set of geographic map limits much more challenging than it should be.
Workaround: There is no general workaround, but here are some suggestions for working with these properties:Even though it seems more complicated, you will probably find it easier to set up your map by specifying the origin and frame limits, rather than setting the map limits.When possible, you can set all your properties in your initial call to axesm (rather than following it with a call to setm).If you do use setm, it sometimes helps to reinitialize related properties that you are not specifying by setting them to [].

Summary: MATLAB? terminates unexpectedly when Microsoft? NetMeeting software is running
Description: While using NetMeeting software, MATLAB might terminate unexpectedly. This is a known problem with the way that MATLAB gets screen bounds. The problem has been observed both on Windows 2000 and Windows XP.
Workaround: For R2006b and later, you can prevent the interactions between NetMeeting and MATLAB by including a java.opts file in your MATLAB startup directory, where the the java.opts file contains the following statement:-Dmatlab.desktop.disableVirtualScreenBounds=trueRestart MATLAB. This will resolve the NetMeeting problem, but there will be problems with dual-monitor configurations.For R14SP3 and R2006a, you can download a patch that prevents interactions with NetMeeting. However, if you use this patch with a dual- monitor configuration, by default MATLAB will only position windows on the primary monitor; you can still drag the windows onto the secondary monitor.To get the patch?Go to matlabroot\java, where matlabroot is the directory in which MATLAB is installed, as determined by running matlabroot in MATLAB.If there is not a patch directory in matlabroot\java, create it.In the patch directory, extract the contents of the attached zip file for your version of MATLAB.Restart MATLAB.

Summary: Test & Measurement Tool generates java error when property lower limit is greater than upper limit
Description: When an instrument driver (MDD-file) has a property with a bounded double constraint in which the minimum bound is greater than the maximum bound, accessing that property in the Test & Measurement Tool generates a java stack trace in the MATLAB Command Window that begins as follows:  Exception in thread "AWT-EventQueue-0" java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: (minimum <= value <= maximum) is false        at javax.swing.SpinnerNumberModel.(Unknown Source)        ...
Workaround: To prevent this error, modify the instrument driver in the MATLAB Instrument Driver Editor (MIDEDIT). Navigate to the property that is causing the error and update the constraint values so that the minimum bound is less than the maximum bound.

Summary: aviread cannot read files larger than 4GB
Description: aviread  returns a blank frame for all images in AVI (.avi) video files larger than 4GB. For example, given a file named largefile.avi , the following code displays a blank frame:   videoFrame = aviread('largefile.avi', 1);  image(videoFrame.cdata);

Summary: Dual monitors on Windows: MATLAB errors when switching between single monitor and dual monitors
Description: On Windows platforms, when you switch from a single monitor to dual monitors (or vice-versa), MATLAB displays this error: Exception in thread "AWT-EventQueue-0" java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: 1at sun.awt.Win32GraphicsEnvironment.getDefaultScreenDevice(Unknown Source) ... The error can also occur when you use Remote Desktop on a machine with a single monitor configuration to access a dual monitor machine.
Workaround: Change the number of active monitors before starting MATLAB using Windows Display Properties > Settings > Display.

Summary: Error when the callback of an object in a GUIDE GUI is triggered continously?while ?loading another GUIDE GUI
Description: For a GUIDE-generated GUI, if some generated callbacks of the figure and/or its child objects are called continously (either explicitly or not)?to load another GUIDE GUI, an error could occur. This happens because the callback code of the current invocation starts to run before the callback code of the previous invocation has finished.
Workaround: To solve this problem, change the value of the Interruptible property of the object whose callback is called continously to 'off' by writing code similar to the following:?set(hObject,'Interruptible','off');where hObject is the handle to the object whose callback is called continously to load another GUIDE GUI. Here is an example of how to do this. Before executing this code, create a blank GUI with GUIDE named gui1.fig and run the code from the same directory it is in; this is the GUI that this code attempts to open via the WindowButtonMotionFcn.function guigui  h = figure;  rectangle('Position', [1 1 1 1]);  set(gca, 'Tag', 'myAxes', ...  'Xlim', [0 10], ...  'Ylim', [0 10]);  handles = guihandles(h);  set(h,'Interruptible','off'); % Will avoid hgload error guidata(h, handles); % Install callback that is triggered continuously set(h, 'WindowButtonMotionFcn', @openOtherGUI); function openOtherGUI(obj, edata);  handles=guidata(obj);  pt = get(handles.myAxes, 'CurrentPoint');  if pt(1,1)>1 && pt(1,1)<2 && pt(1,2)>1 && pt(1,2)<2      gui1; % Open a blank GUI created in GUIDE end

Summary: Discretization of individual GTO, MOSFET, and IGBT blocks
Description: Models containing single forced-commutation power electronic devices (GTO, MOSFET, and IGBT blocks) can not be discretized.
Workaround: To work around this issue, use the Universal Bridge block.

Summary: waitforbuttonpress responds only to mouse button down when used in a figure KeyPressFcn
Description: When used in a figure KeyPressFcn  callback, waitforbuttonpress  resumes program execution only when a mouse button is clicked in the figure window. Pressing a key does not terminate the waitforbuttonpress ; rather, it just re-executes the KeyPressFcn  (and in turn, another waitforbuttonpress ).

Summary: Why is the startup.m code executed when using the MCC command?
Description: I generate a startup banner in my startup.m file that gets displayed when the application is deployed on the target machine. However, this image is also displayed every time I compile my code using the MATLAB Compiler.
Workaround: The MCC command starts a session of MCR to do dependency analysis of the MATLAB code being compiled. In this process, all the startup routines, including startup.m, are executed. This causes the image file to be displayed. To resolve this issue, protect the offending segment of code using the ISMCC test. This prevents the code from being executed during the compilation process. ISMCC will return true when the code is run against the MCR started by the MCC command, false otherwise. The ISMCC function does not take any input arguments and returns only one boolean value.

Summary: Waitbar dialogs have transparent backgrounds
Description: The waitbar dialogs that are displayed to inform you of the progress of lengthy operations in MBC often display with transparent backgrounds. Instead of the normal window grey, they pick up their background from whatever is on the screen when they are displayed. This bug is purely cosmetic and will not cause any data loss.

Summary: Problems when changing map projection with function setm
Description: After calling function setm  to change the 'MapProjection' property of a map axes, especially when switching between an azimuthal and a non-azimuthal projection (e.g., a conic or cylindrical projection), various problems may result: The modified map axes may cover a different part of the Earth. The map frame and graticule may fail to update properly. Map limit properties changed at the same time as the projection may not have the proper effect.
Workaround: You may need to reset various map axes properties such as 'Origin', 'FLatLimit', and 'FLonLimit' after changing projections, as discussed in the section "Switching Between Projections" in the Mapping Toolbox User's Guide.

Summary: Symbol and Marlett fonts not supported in UIcontrol editable text fields
Description: The Symbol and Marlett fonts are not supported in UIcontrols of style EditText. If the fontname of such a control is set to one of these fonts, "unknown character" boxes appear in place of the correct characters.
Workaround: There is no workaround at this time.
